symbols: elevated statue of Ferdinand — the exaltation of the saintly king, triumph of Christian faith over Moorish rule
angels flanking the monument — divine approval and heavenly intercession for the holy ruler
heraldic shields and coats of arms — the union of Castile and Leon under Ferdinand's divinely-guided sovereignty
monumental architecture — the enduring legacy of Ferdinand's piety and the Church Militant on earth
inscriptions 'ALTA FE' — high faith, the virtue that defined Ferdinand's kingship and conquests
description: A detailed preparatory drawing or print depicting an elaborate monumental structure dedicated to St. Ferdinand III of Castile. The composition is symmetrical and vertical, with a statue of Ferdinand standing atop a towering multi-tiered pedestal. Angels and allegorical figures flank each level of the monument. The base features an arched entrance with additional figures and decorative elements. Heraldic shields and coats of arms appear at the bottom. Inscriptions in Latin or Spanish are visible along the upper registers, including what appears to read 'ALTA FE.
theological_description: St. Ferdinand III (1199-1252), King of Castile and Leon, was canonized in 1671 for his role in the Reconquista, recapturing Cordoba and Seville from Moorish rule. The Church venerates him as a model of the rex iustus, the just king whose temporal authority is exercised in submission to divine law. Ferdinand embodies the Old Testament ideal of the godly monarch — like David who united Israel and Josiah who purified worship — a ruler whose military victories are understood as acts of faith rather than mere politics. His devotion to the Blessed Virgin and his deathbed humility (receiving the Eucharist on his knees, begging forgiveness) echo the biblical pattern of the king who acknowledges that all authority comes from God (Rom 13:1). The monumental form itself reflects the theology of memoria — the Church's practice of honoring saints as exemplars whose lives point beyond themselves to the heavenly Jerusalem.

scripture_refs.reason: Governing authority as God's servant — the theological basis for Ferdinand's understanding of Christian kingship.
A prayer for the king to judge with righteousness and defend the cause of the poor — the Davidic ideal Ferdinand sought to embody.
A throne is established through righteousness — the wisdom tradition underlying saintly kingship.
Josiah the reformer-king who purified worship, a biblical parallel to Ferdinand's reconquest for the faith.
Those who through faith conquered kingdoms and administered justice — the catalogue of faithful warriors.
